content: Marie Curie: A Life of Discovery and Determination Marie Curie was born as Maria Sklodowska on November 7, 1867, in Warsaw, Poland. She was the youngest child in a family of five children. Her parents were both teachers who valued education deeply. From an early age, Marie showed exceptional abilities in academics and science. Growing up under Russian occupation was difficult for Poles at that time. The Russians had banned Polish language in schools and universities. Despite these harsh restrictions, Marie excelled in her studies remarkably well. She graduated first in her class in 1883. Due to financial problems and gender restrictions in higher education, Marie could not immediately pursue university studies. She worked as a governess for several years to save money. During this period she also helped fund her older sister's medical studies in Paris. In 1891, Marie finally moved to Paris to study at the famous Sorbonne university. She lived in a small attic room and often had limited food. Her dedication to science was notable, sustained despite severe living conditions. She earned her physics degree in 1893, finishing first in her class again. Marie met Pierre Curie in 1894 while working on magnetic properties of steel. They fell in love and married in 1895, beginning a remarkable partnership. Together they researched the mysterious phenomenon of radioactivity extensively. Marie coined the term "radioactivity," which remains in common scientific use today. Their work with pitchblende ore led to discoveries that reshaped research on radioactive elements. They discovered two new chemical elements: polonium and radium. Polonium was named after Marie's native homeland of Poland as a tribute. The work was extremely dangerous because radiation exposure was not understood then. In 1903, Marie became the first woman to win a Nobel Prize. She shared the Nobel Prize in Physics with Pierre and Henri Becquerel for radioactivity research. Such recognition was rare for a woman in that era. Tragedy struck the Curie family in 1906 when Pierre was killed in a street accident. Marie was devastated by this terrible loss but she continued their important scientific work alone. She took over Pierre's teaching position at the Sorbonne, becoming the first female professor there. In 1911, Marie won her second Nobel Prize, this time in Chemistry for discovering radium. She remains the only person in history to win Nobel Prizes in two different sciences. This achievement remains rare and historically significant. During World War I, Marie made important contributions to the treatment of wounded soldiers. She developed mobile X-ray units called "petites Curies" for battlefield medicine use. She personally drove these vehicles to the front lines to help doctors. She also trained many women to operate the X-ray equipment for medical purposes. Marie Curie's health deteriorated significantly in her later years due to radiation exposure. She continued working in her laboratory despite feeling increasingly unwell and tired. Her dedication to science never wavered even when she was very sick. Marie Curie died on July 4, 1934, from aplastic anemia caused by radiation exposure. Her legacy continues to inspire scientists and women around the world today. The Curie Institutes in Paris and Warsaw continue her groundbreaking research work. Her career illustrates how rigorous training, persistence, and institutional access can expand what scientific research can achieve.
failure_reason: Content rejected: Overall quality score insufficient (6.70 < 9.0). Issues in: Style (6.70 < 8.0)

model_feedback.feedback: The biography is clear and broadly accurate, but it relies on weak intensifiers and inflated phrasing that undermine a professional biographical tone. Several expressions are redundant (“first woman ever…in history”), hyperbolic (“reshaped research,” “extremely dangerous”), or emotionally loaded in a way that reads generic (“devastated by this terrible loss”). There are also minor flow issues from repeated “important/remarkable” judgments instead of concrete detail. Targeted line edits can tighten the prose and remove the most noticeable style problems without changing meaning.
